data.title: AngioDynamics Growing, But With More Noise Than The Street Likes
data.description:
AngioDynamics (ANGO) has been looking more and more like a “two steps forward, one and a half steps back” company. Management has inarguably made progress in building the company’s growth-oriented Medical Tech business and boosted the company’s overall growth profile, but quarter-to-quarter performance has remained erratic and unpredictable, and progress in margin expansion has likewise been unsteady.

AngioDynamics shares are down about 20% since my last update, with two sizable selloffs after the last two quarterly earnings reports tied to growth, margins/reinvestment, and perhaps the pending retirement of the CEO.

At this point I do think AngioDynamics shares are likely undervalued, but this is not a stock for investors who get anxious about short-term market reactions. The Med Tech business does appear to be on solid footing, with sizable addressable markets ahead, but there’s still a lot of work to do before these shares will reliably sustain a better multiple.

Another Quarter of “Mixed” Trends

AngioDynamics’ third quarter offered food for both the bulls and bears, with the latter ultimately winning out on weaker guidance for fourth quarter EBITDA.

Revenue rose 9%, beating by about 2%, with the Med Tech business growing 19% (following 13% growth in FQ2, 26% growth in FQ1, and 22% growth in FQ4’25) and the Medical Devices business growing about 1%.

AlphaVac continues to perform well off of a small base, logging 47% growth on increased usage in pulmonary embolisms. AngioVac saw 5% growth but a small sequential decline. Auryon rose 18%, which is strong against an underlying market growing in the low double-digits, but I’d frankly been hoping that this business would be consistently growing at 20%+ by now. NanoKnife revenue rose 21%, with probe revenue up 20% (and about 4% sequentially), as usage grows in the wake of FDA approval for prostate cancer and a new CPT code that simplifies reimbursement.

Gross margin took a meaningful step back this quarter, with overall gross margin declining 110bp yoy and 350bp qoq largely due to $1.3M in new tariff costs. Med Tech gross margin was stable yoy at 62.5% but down 280bp qoq, while Med Device margin was 44.2% vs. 47.4% a year ago and 49.1% in the prior quarter.

Adjusted EBITDA improved 38% year over year, though the margin remains around 2% (well below most “quality growth” med-techs).

Weak Guidance for Q4, but Not Necessarily for Bad Reasons

All told, revenue performance was pretty good. I’m encouraged by the strong uptake in AlphaVac, though slightly disappointed in Auryon (which has more to do with my expectations/hopes than true underperformance). NanoKnife is a tougher call. It seems churlish to be disappointed with 20%+ growth, but I would like to see more momentum in probes.

On the flip side, there’s work to do on margins. The tariff situation is an “is what it is” situation, particularly as a company of AngioDynamics’ size doesn’t have a lot of mitigation options in the short term. Likewise, there’s just not enough scale in the business yet to fully offset needed reinvestment in SG&A and R&D, though I think more could likely be done to improve (or sell) the remaining Medical Device businesses.

Management’s guidance for the fourth quarter didn’t go over well with the market. While revenue guidance was raised again (and is about 2% higher than the initial guidance for FY’26), and gross margins were guided stable, EBITDA guidance implies a pretty weak fourth quarter.

To that end, management did confirm increased spending, largely on the clinical side. Likewise, management indicated that they’d be using cash to build inventory ahead of some pre-announced business interruptions at the company’s sterilization service provider.

Wall Street is a “what have you done for me lately?” sort of place, but I don’t see this guidance as particularly egregious. Management has the upcoming APEX-RETURN and PAVE studies to fund, both of which could expand the use (and revenue) of AlphaVac and AngioVac, respectively, and there is an ongoing study for Auryon in below-the-knee peripheral atherectomy (AMBITION BTK). I regard these as “takes money to make money” propositions, and arguably the Street should have been expecting an uptick in R&D spending.

As for the inventory build, what is the alternative? It’s impractical to switch sterilization providers on short notice, and it makes sense to me to build inventory ahead of a known disruption as a means of avoiding running out of stock and alienating customers (and/or forcing them to switch to competing products).

The Outlook

My expectations were lower than the Street’s, so not much of note happens with my model at this point. My revenue expectations are a bit higher now, but I’m still looking for around 6% annualized revenue growth over the next five years and long-term growth of over 5%.

Frankly, as the Med Tech business gains scale, I won’t be surprised to see AngioDynamics exit more of its legacy businesses. Between minimal revenue growth and weaker gross margins, there aren’t a lot of arguments for keeping them once the Med Tech business has scaled up.

As far as margins go, this remains a work in progress, particularly given the likelihood (in my view, at least) that management will seek to launch additional studies to expand the labels and marketing appeal of its core growth businesses (AlphaVac, Auryon, and NanoKnife). I think a mid-to-high single-digit EBITDA margin is still possible in FY’27, and I do think a double-digit margin within three years is likewise possible.

Valuation remains challenging, due in large part to the fact that the company isn’t growing fast enough for the Street to ignore the margin situation. With that, the Medical Device business effectively drags down the Med Tech business; I can argue for a $16-$17/share fair value based on a 2x multiple that blends a roughly 4x multiple for the Med Tech business with a sub-1x multiple for Medical Devices.

An upcoming CEO transition is also relevant to a discussion of AngioDynamics’ outlook. CEO Jim Clemmer, who has served for around 10 years now, has announced his intention to retire in FY’27, and the company is now searching for a replacement.

AngioDynamics shares are down about 20% over Clemmer’s tenure, but I would argue the business is on better footing now with the sale of multiple legacy businesses (where the company didn’t have bright growth/margin prospects) and with the repositioning around AngioVac/AlphaVac, Auryon, and NanoKnife. In any case, I’m curious to see who the board selects for the role, and I would expect that those growth businesses will remain the core of the business going forward.

The Bottom Line

My biggest issue with AngioDynamics is honestly the fact that there are a lot of investment options in the med-tech space, and many of them offer less messy, or at least less volatile, growth and value stories. That said, I do think that AngioDynamics is on the right track, and I do think the shares don’t fully reflect the potential of the growth business. For patient investors, I still see a worthwhile case for owning this stock, but that word “patient” is a key consideration here.

data.title: Oracle: OCI And Multicloud Execution Shift The Narrative To Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Since my last coverage, which I rated as Sell, Oracle Corp. (ORCL) stock is down about 24% until 2 April 2026, as investors continue to have concerns about the company’s heavy asset-intensive business model regarding the Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI) business line and its high debt leverage.

ORCL remains with negative FCF, and its backlog depends on OpenAI; however, Q3’26 results showed that the company, on the one hand, reduced OpenAI’s dependency and, on the other hand, proved that the OCI business accelerates the overall growth trajectory, as well as being a key component for cross-selling opportunities leading to customer stickiness.

OCI Accelerates Oracle’s Growth Story via Revenue Acceleration but Also Through Business Restructuring

Oracle’s total revenue rose by 22% YoY to $17.2B in Q3’26, with the main driver being the Cloud segment, which grew by 44%. Specifically, Cloud growth came mainly from its AI infrastructure business, which grew by 84%, while applications grew a solid 13%.

As you can see, ORCL’s total revenue growth rate is strongly correlated with the Cloud segment, which shows a strong growth rate acceleration over the last two years due to high AI demand and, in my opinion, will continue to record higher rates in the following quarters, given the company’s guidance of 46%-50% in Q4’26.

The OCI story does not stop here. According to the CRN report, the OCI segment will have a 75% CAGR, broken down as follows:

  • Revenue of $34 in FY27
  • Revenue of $77B in FY28
  • Revenue of $129B in FY29
  • Revenue of $166B in FY30

Investors remain concerned due to Cloud growth coming with lower profitability, driven by the need for high capital investments mainly for GPUs, CPUs, and memory. Specifically, cloud operating profit of $8.7B in Q3’26 reflects an operating margin of 58%, which is slightly lower than the operating margin of 63% in Q3’25.

In contrast, Hardware and Service segments reported operating margins of 68% and 28% in Q3’26, respectively, compared to 64% and 20% in the previous year. Given that Cloud is the company’s main segment, overall segment-based operating margin reached 56% in Q3’26, down from 59% in Q3’25, but still at high levels.

The company’s overall operating margin increased to 32% in the last quarter, from almost 31% in the previous year, mainly due to cost efficiency improvements and reductions in R&D, administrative, and restructuring expenses.

That said, the company on March 31, 2026, announced layoffs of about 20,000-30,000 employees, which could lead to $8-$10B in free cash flow. I suggest investors should not be concerned about this because Oracle, like many other tech companies, replaced personnel in specific departments with AI tools.

Teams that were affected most are Revenue and Health Sciences (RHS) and SaaS and Virtual Operations Services (SVOS), with at least 30% job cuts per team. RHS is a team that develops software for the health industry, while the SVOS team manages SaaS solutions, such as ERP or HCM. In simple terms, Oracle fired developers and will use AI to produce code -a recently common cost-reduction strategy among many top-tier tech companies.

One more positive sign from the last quarter is the company’s backlog. Specifically, Remaining Performance Obligations (RPO) ended at $553B, up 325% YoY, related to large-scale AI contracts where the company does not expect to raise any additional funds to support these contracts. The key point, in my view, is that ORCL seems to gradually reduce its high concentration on OpenAI with new large companies.

Oracle Shifted Toward a Capital-Heavy IaaS Business Model, but Multicloud (Database) Solutions Could Reach Clients Outside Its Ecosystem

The company shifted its business model from a SaaS asset‑light business model towards a capital‑heavy IaaS one. This shift is reflected in high debt and CapEx, with a direct impact on FCF. Oracle continues to report negative FCF, given its high CapEx of $48B in Q3’26, and the company raised its expectation for the full year from $35B to $50B. I see that ORCL’s high revenue growth is leveraged with high debt -about $162B – due to massive investments, as I mentioned in my previous article.

But in the last quarter, the company stated something that changed my view:

Multicloud Database revenue grew 531% year-over-year. AI infrastructure revenue grew 243% year-over-year. Both also have demand that exceeds supply and a clear execution plan from Oracle that will rapidly turn that demand into profitable recurring revenue. We created our Multicloud partnerships with first Microsoft, then Google and finally, Amazon to bring the best database platform to all clouds… We have global region coverage in all of our partner clouds. We now have 33 regions that live with Microsoft and 14 live with Google. We delivered significant growth with AWS beginning Q3 with 2 AWS regions live, exiting Q3 with 8 AWS regions live, and we will exit Q4 with 22 AWS regions live… as I mentioned earlier, about our Multicloud Database business, that's a much higher margin business, more in the 60% to 80% range.

Let me first explain what Multicloud is based on Oracle's definition:

Multicloud is the coordinated use of cloud services from more than one cloud service provider. By using multiple providers, companies can avoid being tied down to one vendor and maintain their flexibility.

The company also highlights the benefits of using multicloud as follows:

Also, customers can utilize the best product from one cloud service provider and pair it with the best product from another cloud service provider, getting the best‑fit solution.

The key point here is that ORCL offers native cross-cloud solutions. Whereas Microsoft (MSFT) and Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL) offer multicloud solutions by containerized workload mobility, ORCL co-allocates its own hardware -such as CPUs, GPUs, and storage servers - within other vendors' data centers.

Specifically, about Multicloud Database, it allows ORCL’s clients to run Oracle Exadata databases within Azure, Google Cloud, and AWS datacenters, eliminating egress charges, simplifying governance, and enabling high-speed, low-latency communication between OCI and other environments.

In my view, the most important benefit is that Oracle “wins” clients without the need for them to use its own cloud services; therefore, it gains revenue without allocating heavy investments in cloud or datacenters. As a result, the company clients it could not reach otherwise, as they do not use ORCL’s cloud solutions, delivering high margins in the range of 60%–80%. Based on the company, a multicloud win is Air France-KLM, which led to a 13x performance improvement with significantly lower cost for them.

High Concentration and Competition Are the Company’s Main Risks, While Valuation Seems Attractive After a Strong Price Dive

As I mentioned in my last article about Oracle, the company must deal with high concentration risk with OpenAI. It remains questionable how exactly OpenAI will find the capital to support its investments of about $300B in ORCL; as a result, ORCL’s future performance is under question too.

Competition is the other major risk concern. Despite ORCL cooperating with MSFT, GOOG, and AMZN by offering multicloud solutions, competition among them remains heavy, which puts pressure on profit margins.

PE and PEG metrics indicate that ORCL appears undervalued compared to hyperscalers but also compared to promising neo-cloud companies, such as CoreWeave (CRWV) and Nebius (NBIS). Notably, in terms of the PEG ratio, ORCL’s 0.97 value is strongly lower than its main peers.

Also, the company expects revenue of $67B in FY26 and raises total revenue guidance to $90B in FY27, reflecting YoY growth of almost 17% and 34%, respectively, making its FWD P/S ratio even more attractive compared to analyst guidance below.

Bottom Line

In conclusion, Oracle appears undervalued compared to my previous coverage in terms of valuation metrics, and its risk-reward profile seems more attractive to me after Q3’26 results. Investors should weigh up the fact that Oracle currently operates under an asset-heavy business model to offer IaaS solutions due to high AI demand and growth rate acceleration driven by these investments.

I noticed revenue growth rate acceleration over the last quarters, and I anticipate this rate to expand further. Overall, ORCL's risk-reward profile is attractive at current price levels, with asymmetric reward if the company expands its growth rate in the following quarters.

Based on all the above, I upgraded the stock from Sell to Buy, but I suggest investors hold a small position of ORCL in their portfolio, probably as a supportive high-risk, high-reward stock among other cloud stock companies.

data.title: Mid-America: A Dirt-Cheap Way To Buy Apartments
data.description:
Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA) is an apartment REIT that owns class A assets in growing markets and that aren't encumbered with much debt. Excess supply and interest rate fears have caused the stock to fall, which is giving investors a nice opportunity to purchase the underlying apartments at an attractive discount to their true worth.

Introduction

Mid-America Apartments is a residential REIT with a portfolio of approximately 103,000 apartments spread mostly across the Sunbelt region of the United States. About three-quarters of the portfolio is located in large cities and their suburbs, while about a quarter of the units are located in mid-tier markets.

Large markets include metros like Atlanta, Dallas, Orlando, and Tampa Bay, while mid-tier markets include smaller cities like Charleston, Greenville, Fredericksburg, and Richmond.

The portfolio mostly consists of garden-style apartments, along with a good chunk of mid-rise units. Garden-style apartments have three stories or less, while mid-rise apartments are 4-9 stories. A high-rise is anything 10 stories or higher.

While the portfolio is diverse with exposure in many different markets, the REIT is fairly concentrated in its top five markets. More than 40% of net operating income (NOI) comes from these metro areas.

There are a few reasons why MAA has focused on the Sunbelt region of the United States. These areas are growing faster than most other areas of the country. Salaries are relatively high -- especially in cities -- and state income taxes are lower. That translates into more dollars in a tenant's pocket. Unemployment is low. The weather is nice. And the real estate itself is cheaper than most other parts of the country.

MAA specifically has an average lease-to-income level of around 20%, which is quite affordable.

Put it all together, and the Sunbelt offers a good deal for both the tenant and the landlord.

Renting is also compelling versus owning. Since the beginning of 2020, the average new lease price of an MAA apartment is 23% higher. Average rents went from $1,267 per month to $1,555. The estimated mortgage payment is 99% higher, increasing from $1,058 per month to $2,107. It's easy to see why tenants would choose to continue renting versus becoming homeowners.

I'll also mention the REIT's excellent balance sheet. Mid-America has a debt-to-assets ratio of 30.2%, which is one of the best balance sheets in the sector. Debt maturities are spread out as well, which reduces the risk of any interest rate shock upon debt refinancing. Most of the debt is fixed rate, too.

This is a company that is built to last, which is an attractive feature in today's uncertain markets.

And yet, despite all MAA has going for it, Mid-America shares have been weak over the last year, losing approximately 25% of their value. The stock is down almost 50% versus all-time highs set in the early part of 2022, too.

Investors are concerned about one main thing. Developers have been busy building for the last few years -- especially in the Sunbelt -- as they took advantage of low interest rates and an attractive outlook in the 2021-22 period. These apartments are now completed and competing with existing MAA units. This oversupply has temporarily pushed rents lower in a lot of different markets.

I believe this short-term weakness is a long-term investor's opportunity. Mid-America shares are cheap in a few different ways, especially when we break them down on a per-apartment basis.

Price Per Apartment

Let's do some quick math and figure out just how cheap Mid-America Properties is by breaking it down on a per-unit basis.

Then, we'll compare that number to the replacement value and see what kind of deal investors are getting today.

As of December 31st, 2005, Mid-American Properties had 102,814 completed apartment units in the portfolio. It also has a development program with further apartments in various stages of completion, but for the sake of this exercise, we'll ignore those properties. We'll also ignore MAA's 35% ownership in a Washington, D.C. property.

We'll use a very simple formula to figure out the value per unit. We'll add the company's market cap plus its debt (including preferred shares) to get the total value of the portfolio. After all, that's what an investor is paying for it today.

We'll then take that number and divide it by the number of units.

As I type this, MAA has a market cap of $14.7B. It owes $4.4B in long-term debt and $299.5M in short-term debt. This debt is offset by $60M in cash. This gives us an enterprise value of $19.34B.

(Note: all figures come from Seeking Alpha's balance sheet page)

Divide this by 102,814 units, and we get a value per suite of just over $188,000.

Now that we have a number, a bigger question emerges. Is this actually cheap?

I argue it is for a few different reasons. First, let's take a look at MAA's own development portfolio. The company has 3,245 units in various stages of completion in seven different markets. These are expected to cost it $1.15B to complete, for an average cost of $355,000 per unit. That's nearly twice as expensive on a per-unit basis as the implied value investors are getting from buying the stock.

We can also look at MAA's own recent acquisition history to see what management thinks is a fair price to pay on a per-unit basis. Over the last two years, Mid-America has acquired four different communities, spending $370.5M to acquire 1,320 units. That works out to $280,681 per unit, or about a 5.5% cap rate. That's about 50% higher than the implied value of MAA's current portfolio.

Finally, let's value the entire MAA portfolio just like a real estate investor would value a rental property or an apartment complex. We'll look at the cap rate.

In 2025, MAA generated $1.37B in NOI. As mentioned above, the portfolio is worth $19.34B. That gives us a cap rate of 7.1%. According to CBRE, typical cap rates in the Sunbelt part of the United States are between 4.5% and 5%. Investors are getting a nice deal by purchasing MAA shares versus the underlying real estate privately.

Whether we value MAA on a per-apartment-unit basis, a discount-to-replacement-value basis, or a cap rate basis, the conclusion is the same. The stock is cheap versus buying apartment units directly.

Traditional Valuation

Next, let's value MAA as we would any other stock -- on a multiple of funds from operations.

The REIT came out with 2026 guidance a few weeks ago that was admittedly a little weak. Management projected that FFO would be a little less than last year's number, as the company still sees some weakness in a few of its markets. FFO is expected to be $8.35 to $8.71 per share, for a midpoint of $8.53 per share.

Analysts tend to agree with that assessment. The Seeking Alpha earnings page says analysts expect $8.56 per share in FFO in 2026. That number is expected to increase to $8.73 per share in 2027.

That puts us at 14.6x 2026's FFO and 14.3x 2027's FFO. I believe that's cheap for a REIT that offers dependable earnings and has such a strong balance sheet.

Another factor we can analyze to see MAA's valuation is the dividend yield. This is a simple test that looks at how much dividend income the investment will generate versus the company's history. A high dividend yield often indicates the stock is undervalued, while a lower dividend yield can signal the opposite.

The evidence here is clear. Mid-America's shares are cheap on a dividend yield basis. The current yield is just over 5%, which is the company's highest payout in over a decade.

Despite the larger than normal dividend yield, investors can count on the payout. The payout ratio is around 70% of 2026's projected FFO, and the company has a 15-year dividend growth streak it will want to project. We may see anemic dividend growth for a year or two as Mid-America makes its way through this rough patch, but dividend growth should resume at a higher rate in a couple of years.

Risks

Investors are worried about two main things here.

The first is apartment supply. Apartment starts have slowed significantly in most of MAA's markets, but there are still units that are expected to hit the market in 2026 and 2027. Long-term the situation should be fine, but there could be more weakness in the short-term. Especially if the economy struggles.

The other risk is interest rates. If rates continue to spike, then every apartment REIT will suffer as they renew existing debt or are forced to borrow more. Interest is one of a REIT's biggest costs; any increase there will flow straight to the bottom line. And not in a good way, either.

The Bottom Line

I believe MAA shares are cheap no matter how you slice it. You're buying unloved assets that are working through a very fixable problem. There is a glut of new supply currently on the market, but that will correct itself in a year or two as folks continue migrating to the Sunbelt.

Shares are cheap on a cap rate basis or on a per-unit basis. And they're quite cheap when you compare them to replacement value.

MAA is well-equipped to make it through even the worst downturn unscathed. Residential apartments tend to hold up well during recessions -- after all, everybody needs a place to live -- and it has a fortress balance sheet. It should be able to bring its new development projects online while maintaining that balance sheet strength, too.

I've been adding this one to my portfolio. I rate shares a buy.

data.title: Treasury Yields Snapshot: April 2, 2026
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The yield on the 10-year note finished April 2, 2026, at 4.31%. Meanwhile, the 2-year note ended at 3.79%, and the 30-year yield ended at 4.88%.

The chart below overlays the daily performance of several Treasury bonds, starting from the pre-recession equity market peaks, along with the Federal Funds Rate (FFR) since 2007.

This next table shows the highs and lows of yields and the Federal Funds Rate since 2007.

A Long-Term Look at the 10-Year Treasury Yield

Here is a long-term view of the 10-year yield starting in 1965, well before the 1973 oil embargo that triggered the era of 'stagflation' (economic stagnation coupled with inflation)

Inverted Yield Curve

An inverted yield curve is when longer-term Treasury yields are lower than their shorter-term counterparts. The next chart displays the latest 10-2 spread. Typically, the spread turns negative for a period before rising again prior to recessions, as illustrated in the four recessions shown on this chart. For this reason, the 10-2 spread is widely considered a reliable leading indicator for recessions. The lead time between a negative spread and the onset of a recession varies, with recessions beginning anywhere from 18 to 92 weeks after the spread goes negative.

One false positive is seen in 1998, where the spread briefly went negative without leading to a recession. In the case of the 2009 recession, the spread went negative multiple times before rising again. Most recently, the spread was continuously negative from July 5, 2022, to August 26, 2024. The last time the spread was negative was on September 5, 2024.

If we consider the first negative spread date as the starting point, the average lead time to a recession is 48 weeks, or about eleven months. If we instead use the last positive spread date before a recession, the average lead time is 18.5 weeks, or about 4.25 months.

For another perspective on the yield curve, the 10-3mo spread below uses an even shorter-term maturity. The lead time to recessions based on this spread (after it turns negative) ranges from 34 to 69 weeks. Like the 10-2 spread, we see the same false positive in 1998, as well as multiple instances of the spread turning negative before rising again ahead of the 2009 recession. Recently, the spread was negative from October 25, 2022, to December 12, 2024. Since February 26th, the spread has swung back and forth from positive to negative territory.

If we consider the first negative spread date as the starting point, the average lead time to a recession is 48 weeks, or about eleven months. If we instead use the last positive spread date after the spread had been negative, the average lead time is 13 weeks, or about three months.

The 30-Year Fixed Rate Mortgage

The Federal Funds Rate influences the cost of borrowing for banks. When the Fed increases this rate, banks often raise their lending rates, which can impact mortgage rates, among other things. Therefore, a rising FFR generally leads to higher mortgage rates, and vice versa. However, this was not the case recently when the Fed began their rate-cutting cycle in September 2024 and mortgage rates moved in the opposite direction. With that said, mortgage rates have been on the decline as of late, following a similar fashion of the FFR. The latest Freddie Mac Weekly Primary Mortgage Market Survey put the 30-year fixed rate at 6.46%, its highest level since September.

Now let's see the 10-year against the S&P 500 with some notes on Federal Reserve intervention. Fed policy has been a major influence on market behavior.

ETFs associated with Treasuries include: Vanguard 0-3 Month Treasury Bill ETF (VBIL), Vanguard Intermediate-Term Treasury ETF (VGIT), and Vanguard Long-Term Treasury ETF (VGLT).

data.title: GNR: A Beneficiary Of Rising Energy Prices
data.description:
In a year when most ETFs have posted noticeable losses from the first quarter, the performance of the State Street SPDR S&P Global Natural Resources ETF (GNR) certainly stands out in the crowd. The fund has gained over 20% in the first three months of 2025. As with most funds of its kind, investors can use GNR to benefit from rising commodity prices, especially the prices of natural resources like oil, agricultural products, and precious metals. With oil prices up over 40% in the last month alone, many of the stocks held by GNR have climbed in response.

How long these recent trends will last is anyone’s guess at this point. An immediate end to the Middle Eastern conflict is not yet in sight, but few expect military action in Iran to extend into the second half of this year. I have written an analysis for two other natural resource ETFs in the last few months. Last December, I wrote about the VanEck Natural Resources ETF (HAP). This January, I added commentary on the American Beacon GLG Natural Resources ETF (MGNR), noting its heavy allocation toward precious metals and mining stocks.

Each of these funds has a slightly different investment approach within the same general category of natural resources. GNR appears to be in the right place at the right time, currently, but the cyclicality of its holdings, especially in the energy sector, will likely bring it back toward the mean in the months ahead. I consider the fund to be only a Hold, in spite of its strong start to 2026.

ETF Overview

The State Street SPDR S&P Global Nat Resources ETF tracks the results that correspond to the total return performance of the S&P® Global Natural Resources Index. This index is made up of 90 of the largest global companies that are engaged in the businesses related to natural resources and commodities. These 90 companies are selected from three “sub-indices” - the S&P Global Natural Resources - Agriculture Index, the S&P Global Natural Resources - Energy Index and the S&P Global Natural Resources - Metals and Mining Index. Each of these has a weight of one-third of the larger index that is followed by GNR. No stock can have an allocation in the broader index of more than 5%. The index is reconstituted on the last trading day in August and rebalanced in February, May, and November.

As of the close of the market on April 2, GNR has $4.9 billion in assets under management. The top 10 equity holdings in the fund are shown below with a comparison to the allocation found in the ETF's benchmark index. Two stocks—Exxon (XOM) and Shell plc (SHEL)—have weights above 5% because they have increased in value since the last time the ETF’s holdings were rebalanced.

In total, these 10 companies listed comprise 36.3% of the fund’s assets. They include four oil giants, four companies in the mining business, and two involved in the agriculture industry. Company number three on the list, Nutrien Ltd. (NTR) is a name that may be new to many investors. A Canadian provider of chemicals, fertilizer, and agricultural services, NTR has a market cap of $35.9 billion and has gained just over 50% in the last 12 months. Another less well-known name, UPM-Kymmene Oyj (UPMMY), is a Finnish producer of timber and paper products. Its stock has produced a total return of 19.1% over the last year.

As one would expect with almost any natural resources ETF, the sector allocation for GNR is primarily concentrated in basic materials and energy. Materials hold 56.4% of the fund’s assets. Energy stocks have an allocation of 38.2%. Consumer staples have a weight of 4.6%. The fund shows a 0.82% allocation to the real estate sector, which is entirely based on its ownership of one company, Weyerhaeuser (WY), a REIT that is one of the world’s largest owners of timber.

GNR is truly a global ETF, with almost two-thirds of its assets invested outside the US. Almost 20% of the companies owned by the fund are based in Canada. An additional 11.8% are within the borders of the United Kingdom. The list below shows each of the 13 countries that have a representation in GNR of at least 1%, but the total number of nations represented in the portfolio is 21 as of March 31.

The State Street SPDR S&P Global Natural Resources ETF has a current dividend yield of 2.30%. Distributions are made twice each year, with the next payout due to hit investors’ accounts in early June. The $1.72 per share paid out in FY 2025 was 27.1% less than the $2.36 distributed in FY 2024. As shown in the graph below, the fund’s current yield is its lowest since Q2 2021.

The fund’s expense ratio of 0.40% may not sound great to investors who are accustomed to the single-digit fees charged by larger, more traditional US equity ETFs. However, for a global fund that invests in just a few market sectors in over 20 countries, the ratio charged by State Street seems much more reasonable. It is lower than the expenses reported from the two other natural resource ETFs that I have covered in the last five months. The VanEck Natural Resources ETF charges investors 0.42%. The American Beacon GLG Natural Resources ETF has an expense ratio of 0.75%.

Analysis of GNR

The returns from GNR over the last several years have been impressive, regardless of its sector allocations. The State Street website only has the fund's returns updated through the end of February, but as can be seen below, investors in the ETF have been able to enjoy double-digit returns on average for the last decade. Even when tax considerations are made, GNR has been able to produce annual gains of over 10% dating back to early 2017.

The fund’s valuation and growth metrics that are given on Morningstar paint a somewhat mixed picture of the prospects for the fund moving forward. When compared to the average in the natural resources category, GNR’s holdings have lower PE, price-to-book, and price-to-cash flow ratios. However, they also have lower long-term earnings expectations, lower cash flow growth, and negative sales growth.
